# Synthetic biology strategies for sustainable bioplastic production by yeasts

### Affiliations

# Synthetic biology strategies for sustainable bioplastic production by yeasts

### Authors

### Affiliations

## Abstract

The increasing environmental concerns regarding conventional plastics have led to a growing demand for sustainable alternatives, such as biodegradable plastics. Yeast cell factories, specifically Saccharomyces cerevisiae and Yarrowia lipolytica, have emerged as promising platforms for bioplastic production due to their scalability, robustness, and ease of manipulation. This review highlights synthetic biology approaches aimed at developing yeast cell factories to produce key biodegradable plastics, including polylactic acid (PLA), polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs), and poly (butylene adipate-co-terephthalate) (PBAT). We explore recent advancements in engineered yeast strains that utilize various synthetic biology strategies, such as the incorporation of new genetic elements at the gene, pathway, and cellular system levels. The combined efforts of metabolic engineering, protein engineering, and adaptive evolution have enhanced strain efficiency and maximized product yields. Additionally, this review addresses the importance of integrating computational tools and machine learning into the Design-Build-Test-Learn cycle for strain development. This integration aims to facilitate strain development while minimizing effort and maximizing performance. However, challenges remain in improving strain robustness and scaling up industrial production processes. By combining advanced synthetic biology techniques with computational approaches, yeast cell factories hold significant potential for the sustainable and scalable production of bioplastics, thus contributing to a greener bioeconomy.

**Keywords:**
PBAT; PHA; PLA; bioplastics; synthetic biology; yeast.

# Synthetic Biology Boosts the Biological Depolymerization and Upcycling of Waste Plastic Resources

Received: 27 January 2025
Accepted: 24 February 2025
Published: 28 February 2025

![Creative Commons](/style/image/iconby.png)

ôˋ 2025 The authors. This is an open access article under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (<https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/>).

## 1. Introduction

![](/uploads/2025/02/28/6b4db9eea739a685ae953594b36c8d59.jpg)

## 2. Research Progress on Plastic Biodegradation

PET plastic is a polymer compound formed by the ester bond linkage of terephthalic acid (TPA) and ethylene glycol (EG), typically existing in amorphous and semi-crystalline forms. It is mainly used in packaging materials such as beverage bottles [[11](#B11)]. Microorganisms degrade PET plastic by secreting extracellular depolymerases that hydrolyze the ester bonds in PET, leading to depolymerization. The resulting small molecular depolymerized products can then be further mineralized by the microorganisms into water and CO2 [[12](#B12)].

Crystallinity is one of the key factors influencing the biological depolymerization of PET. The higher the crystallinity, the more difficult the depolymerization becomes. Therefore, researchers typically use amorphous or low-crystallinity PET films as model substrates for screening PET-degrading microorganisms and studying their depolymerization characteristics. Reported PET-degrading microorganisms include *Fusarium solani* [[13](#B13),[14](#B14)], *Humicola insolens* [[15](#B15)], *Thermomonospora fusca* [[16](#B16),[17](#B17),[18](#B18),[19](#B19)], *Streptomyces viridis* [[20](#B20)], *Brucella intermedia* IITR130 [[21](#B21)], among others. However, most of these microorganisms only degrade and modify the surface of PET, and their ability to degrade actual PET plastic waste is quite limited. In 2016, Yoshida et al. discovered a bacterium, *I. sakaiensis* 201-F6, which was capable of fully degrading low-crystallinity PET films after 6 weeks at 30 ô¯C. This bacterium is currently regarded as the most efficient in PET degradation among those reported to date [[22](#B22)]. Recently, *Gordonia* sp. CN2K has been shown to grow using PET as the sole carbon source, and it can degrade 40.43% of PET film within a 45-day incubation period [[23](#B23)].

Due to the high glass transition temperature of PET plastic, directly using enzymes to depolymerize PET at high temperatures (60ã70 ô¯C) has become a research hotspot in recent years. Various types of enzyme components with PET depolymerization activity have been discovered, including lipases, esterases, and keratinases, with keratinase being one of the most studied and efficient PET depolymerizing enzymes [[24](#B24)] ([Table 2](#Table2)). Keratinase TfH from *T. fusca* DSM43793 causes a 50% loss in the mass of a PET film with a crystallinity of 10% under catalytic conditions at 55 ô¯C within 3 weeks [[25](#B25)]. Keratinase HiC from *Humicola insolens* almost completely degrades low-crystallinity PET films under conditions of 70 ô¯C within 96 h. It is currently the most active and thermally stable fungal-derived polyester hydrolase reported [[26](#B26)]. Keratinase LCC, derived from plant compost, can degrade 25% of amorphous PET films after 24 h of catalysis at 70 ô¯C. This enzyme shows a certain degree of homology to TfH polyester hydrolase [[27](#B27)]. PETase from *Deinococcus maricopensis* was found to be comparable to LCCICCG at 50 ô¯C in degrading semi-crystalline sections of post-consumer PET bottles, but it appeared to be less sensitive to crystallinity degree increase [[28](#B28)]. Additionally, lipases from *Thermomyces insolens*, *Candida antarctica*, and *Aspergillus* sp., as well as esterases from *Cladosporium* and *Cladosporioides*, *Melanocarpus albomyces*, and *Penicillium citrinum*, have some depolymerization effects on PET. However, these enzymes mainly increase the hydrophilicity of the PET surface and cause changes in its surface morphology, without significantly degrading the material.

PU plastic is a polymer formed by the condensation of three components: isocyanates, polyols, and chain extenders, which contain repeating urethane bonds. PU is a semi-crystalline thermosetting plastic, where isocyanates form its crystalline portion, known as the hard segment of PU, which determines the hardness and tensile strength of the material. Polyols and chain extenders make up the amorphous portion, known as the soft segment of PU, which governs the elasticity and elongation properties of PU. The biodegradation process of PU primarily occurs through the cleavage of ester bonds in the soft segments, leading to depolymerization. As a result, most microorganisms reported to degrade PU are focused on the degradation of polyester-based PU, with fewer reports on the degradation of polyether-based PU [[29](#B29)]. Several microorganisms, such as *Fusarium* sp., *Curvularia* sp., *Cladosporium* sp., *Penicillium* sp., *Bacillus* sp., *Pseudomonas* sp., and *Streptomyces* sp., have been confirmed to possess the ability to degrade PU plastics, as shown in [Table 1](#Table1). ûlvarez-BarragûÀn et al. [[30](#B30)] isolated six *Cladosporium* sp. strains, which were able to degrade 75ã85% of water-based PU (Impranil DLN) within two weeks, showing a high degradation level for polyester-based PU. *Aspergillus* sp. is a type of fungus reported to degrade polyester-based PU. *Aspergillus flavus*, isolated from landfill soil, could use polyester PU films as the sole carbon source and degrade 60.6% of the polyester PU within 30 days [[31](#B31)]. Another *Aspergillus* sp. S45, isolated from a solid waste disposal site, could degrade 20% of polyester PU films in 28 days [[32](#B32)]. *Aspergillus tubingensis*, isolated from a landfill by Khan et al. [[33](#B33)], was able to degrade polyester PU films into fragments after 21 days of incubation in an inorganic salt liquid medium containing 2% glucose. Additionally, the microbial strain isolated from soil responsible for the maximum degradation of 55% of polyurethane films was identified as *Aspergillus versicolor* (ARF5) [[34](#B34)]. *Streptomyces* sp. PU10, isolated from soil contaminated with polyurethane, demonstrated the remarkable ability to degrade a soluble polyester-PU (Impranil) at various temperatures, achieving more than 96% degradation of 10 g/L within 48 h [[35](#B35)]. However, research on the use of these microorganisms to degrade real-world polyester PU plastic waste is still limited [[36](#B36)]. Nearly, a 42.1% weight loss of PU foam was observed after 30 days of exposure to *Bacillus* sp. YXP1, a bacterium isolated from a plastic landfill. This indicates that strain YXP1 exhibits exceptional degradation capabilities for PU foam [[37](#B37)].

**[Table 1](#Table1)**.

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| PET | *Fusarium solani* | PET fibers | 30 | Surface modification of PET fibers | [[13](#B13),[14](#B14)] |
| *Humicola insolens* | PET fibers | 30 | Surface modification of PET fibers | [[15](#B15)] |

| *Saccharomonospora viridis* | PET fibers | 30 | Surface modification of PET fibers | [[20](#B20)] |
| *Ideonella sakaiensis* 201-F6 | PET film | 30 | Complete degradation of low crystallinity PET film within 6 weeks | [[22](#B22)] |

| PU | *Aspergillus flavus*  (ITCC 6051) | PU film | 28 ôÝ 2 | Degradation of 60.6% polyester PU film within 30 days | [[31](#B31)] |
| *Aspergillus tubingensis* | PU film | 37 | Degradation of polyester PU film into fragments in 14 days | [[33](#B33)] |

| *Cladosporium pseudocladosporioides, Cladosporium tenuissimum,*   *Cladosporium asperulatum,*  *Cladosporium montecillanum,*  *Aspergillus fumigatus,*  *Penicillium chrysogenum* | PU film | 25ã30 | Degradation of 10% to 65% polyester PU film in 21 days | [[30](#B30)] |

Biological enzymes can depolymerize PU plastics by hydrolyzing the ester or urethane bonds. Common enzyme types include esterases, ureases, and proteases [[38](#B38)]. Esterases are currently the most effective enzymes for PU plastic degradation. For example, the esterase PudA is derived from *Comamonas acidovorans* TB-35 [[39](#B39),[40](#B40)], and esterases PueA, PueB, and PulA from *Pseudomonas* species [[41](#B41),[42](#B42)]. Although these cloned esterases can effectively depolymerize water-based polyurethane (Impranil DLN), they are largely ineffective against real PU waste. Enzyme (MTL) isolated from the gut bacteria of mealworms that consume plastic has the ability to hydrolyze thermoplastic films (PEGA-HDI) and thermosetting foams. It has been shown to possess superior PU plastic degradation capabilities compared to PueA, PueB, and PulA [[43](#B43)]. Papain, a protease, was able to break down PU films after being treated at 37 ô¯C for 1 to 6 months. GPC and FTIR analysis showed some degree of carbamate bond cleavage [[44](#B44)]. Similarly, öÝ-chymotrypsin could reduce the average molecular weight of PU by over 30% after 10 days of reaction at 25 ô¯C [[45](#B45)]. Research on urease-mediated PU degradation is relatively scarce. Phua et al. [[44](#B44)] found that urease (EC 3.5.1.5) could degrade PU, with analysis via GPC and infrared showing breakdown primarily due to the hydrolysis of urethane bonds in PU. Yannick Branson and others discovered three urethanases (UMG-SP-1 to UMG-SP-3, GenBank accession codes: OP972509, OP972510, and OP972511). These urethanases were found from a soil-derived metagenomic library and were shown to effectively hydrolyze the urethane bonds in polyether-based polyurethanes [[46](#B46)]. Additionally, Schmidt et al. [[47](#B47)] discovered that keratinases LCC, TfCut2, Tcur1278, and Tcur0390, which degrade PET plastics, also exhibited some degradation of polyester-based PU. After 100 h of degradation at 70 ô¯C, the PU plastic degradation rate reached 0.3ã3.2%. Recently, cutinase PlCut1 [[48](#B48)] and CpCut1 [[49](#B49)] have demonstrated a strong ability to degrade PU, with CpCut1 showing particularly impressive results. It was capable of degrading 40.5% of thermoplastic PU film and 20.6% of post-consumer foam, while completely depolymerizing Impranil DLN-SD.

# How synthetic biology could help degrade plastic waste

![](https://www.polytechnique-insights.com/wp-content/themes/Starter/front/dist/img/ic-eye.png)
![](https://www.polytechnique-insights.com/wp-content/themes/Starter/front/dist/img/ic-eye.gif)
![Tania Louis](https://www.polytechnique-insights.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/tania-louis-e1652972105750-380x480.png)

After a year of stagôÙnaôÙtion due to the panôÙdemôÙic in 2020, globôÙal plasôÙtics proôÙducôÙtion has startôÙed to rise again: 390 milôÙlion tonnes were proôÙduced in 2021, 90% of which was proôÙduced from fosôÙsil resources[1](#note-1). Once used, the majorôÙiôÙty is likeôÙly to end up as waste, adding to the 5 bilôÙlion tonnes that had already accuôÙmuôÙlatôÙed in 2015, repôÙreôÙsentôÙing 79% of all plasôÙtic waste proôÙduced by humanôÙiôÙty[2](#note-2). The enviôÙronôÙmenôÙtal probôÙlems posed by these mateôÙriôÙals are numerôÙous. Could micro-organôÙisms help solveô them?

## **Natural plastic eaters**

Although plasôÙtics are polôÙluôÙtants for the vast majorôÙiôÙty of livôÙing beings, some bacôÙteôÙria and funôÙgi have acquired the abilôÙiôÙty to turn them into an enerôÙgy source. Found in the soil, the sea or the intestines of some aniôÙmals, sevôÙerôÙal species of micro-organôÙisms can attack difôÙferôÙent types of plasôÙtics (PET, PP, PS, PE, PUR, PLAãÎ) thanks to enzymes that alter these long hydroôÙcarôÙbon chains[3](#note-3). HowôÙevôÙer, this does not mean that simôÙply putting them in conôÙtact with the right plasôÙtic will make it disappear.

![](https://www.polytechnique-insights.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/03/adobestock_210575546-1024x768.jpeg)

The process is most effecôÙtive when the plasôÙtics have already been damôÙaged, for examôÙple by UV light or chemôÙiôÙcal treatôÙments, and when the micro-organôÙisms are placed under optiôÙmal pH and temôÙperôÙaôÙture conôÙdiôÙtions[4](#note-4). HowôÙevôÙer, each species is only active on cerôÙtain plasôÙtics and being able to attack a mateôÙrôÙiôÙal does not imply being able to degrade it comôÙpleteôÙly. Even the strains that have attractôÙed the most attenôÙtion, such as IdeonelôÙla sakaienôÙsis, a bacôÙteriôÙum that breaks down PET into its conôÙstituent monomers[5](#note-5), have a major limôÙiôÙtaôÙtion: they need a few weeks or even months to degrade limôÙitôÙed quanôÙtiôÙties of plasôÙtics. In othôÙer words, these plasôÙtic-eatôÙing micro-organôÙisms are not an ideôÙal soluôÙtion for manôÙagôÙing our polôÙluôÙtion. But they could still be of great help toô us!

## **Optimising what already exists**

Progress in genetôÙics, in terms of underôÙstandôÙing the mechôÙaôÙnisms involved, anticôÙiôÙpatôÙing posôÙsiôÙble results, and develôÙopôÙing molôÙeôÙcôÙuôÙlar tools, now makes it posôÙsiôÙble to volôÙunôÙtarôÙiôÙly modôÙiôÙfy genomes ã in parôÙticôÙuôÙlar to make organôÙisms synôÙtheôÙsise cerôÙtain types of proôÙtein. Many bioôÙlogôÙiôÙcal research projects now involve the proôÙducôÙtion of cells or even organôÙisms that over- or under-express cerôÙtain genes. The genetôÙic modôÙiôÙfiôÙcaôÙtions that make it posôÙsiôÙble to obtain them are almost a craft, with each researcher makôÙing what they need. But this approach can be adaptôÙed to a comôÙpleteôÙly difôÙferôÙentô scale!

Since 2000, synôÙthetôÙic biolôÙoôÙgy has proôÙposed applyôÙing engiôÙneerôÙing methôÙods to molôÙeôÙcôÙuôÙlar biolôÙoôÙgy. It conôÙsidôÙers genes and othôÙer DNA sequences (espeôÙcialôÙly regôÙuôÙlaôÙtoôÙry ones) as buildôÙing blocks that can be optiôÙmised and comôÙbined in a metaôÙbolôÙic engiôÙneerôÙing logôÙic. The new bioôÙlogôÙiôÙcal synôÙtheôÙsis pathôÙways thus devised can then be implantôÙed in cells, genôÙerôÙalôÙly micro-organôÙisms, which become small genetôÙiôÙcalôÙly modôÙiôÙfied proôÙducôÙtion facôÙtoôÙries. This approach has its limôÙiôÙtaôÙtions, the main one being the chaotôÙic comôÙplexôÙiôÙty of livôÙing organôÙisms. A synôÙthetôÙic pathôÙway that seems optiôÙmal in theôÙoôÙry does not always work in pracôÙtice, once conôÙfrontôÙed with the realôÙiôÙty of a celôÙlãs enviôÙronôÙment. And the tranôÙsiôÙtion to a largôÙer scale, which is essenôÙtial for many appliôÙcaôÙtions, conôÙstiôÙtutes an addiôÙtionôÙal levôÙel of difôÙfiôÙculôÙty: livôÙing sysôÙtems often remain senôÙsiôÙtive and unpredictable.

![](https://www.polytechnique-insights.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/03/screenshot-2023-03-07-at-12-13-07-1024x574.png)

NevôÙerôÙtheôÙless, in the last twenôÙty years, synôÙthetôÙic biolôÙoôÙgy has become much more than a theôÙoôÙretôÙiôÙcal vision. Many comôÙpaôÙnies are investôÙing in this approach, which has already led to the comôÙmerôÙcialôÙiôÙsaôÙtion of varôÙiôÙous prodôÙucts in fields as diverse as medôÙiôÙcine, food, and mateôÙriôÙals[7](#note-7). Fuelled by biotechôÙnoôÙlogôÙiôÙcal advances such as DNA synôÙtheôÙsis, high-throughôÙput sequencôÙing, and new gene-editôÙing techôÙniques, supôÙportôÙed by increasôÙingôÙly powôÙerôÙful comôÙputôÙer tools and inteôÙgratôÙing new knowlôÙedge strucôÙtured in increasôÙingôÙly rich dataôÙbasôÙes, it seems reaôÙsonôÙable to expect synôÙthetôÙic biolôÙoôÙgy to proôÙduce breakôÙthroughs[8](#note-8).

## **Attacking plastics**

MicroorôÙganôÙisms capaôÙble of degradôÙing plasôÙtics may not be effiôÙcient enough to be useôÙful on a large scale, but their study proôÙvides new ammuôÙniôÙtion for synôÙthetôÙic biolôÙoôÙgy! Each enzyme disôÙcovôÙered enrichôÙes the catôÙaôÙlogue of tools availôÙable to design and optiôÙmise metaôÙbolôÙic pathôÙways. And the modôÙiôÙfiôÙcaôÙtion of these proôÙteins after studyôÙing their strucôÙture someôÙtimes makes it posôÙsiôÙble to obtain even more effiôÙcient verôÙsions of them, only a few years after their disôÙcovôÙery[9](#note-9)[10](#note-10).

Unlike othôÙer recyôÙcling methôÙods, which involve a loss of mateôÙrôÙiôÙal qualôÙiôÙty, the bioôÙlogôÙiôÙcal degraôÙdaôÙtion pathôÙways allow the conôÙstituent monomers of the plasôÙtics to be returned. These can then be reassemôÙbled to proôÙduce a prodôÙuct equivôÙaôÙlent to new, with no conôÙstraints on the colours or types of objects that can be manôÙuôÙfacôÙtured. HowôÙevôÙer, there are still probôÙlems to overôÙcome. On the one hand, this deconôÙstrucôÙtion of plasôÙtics releasôÙes the addiôÙtives added to these mateôÙriôÙals, which must be manôÙaged on their own. On the othôÙer hand, these approachôÙes remain more expenôÙsive than proôÙducôÙtion from fosôÙsil resources, and incenôÙtives will be needôÙed to push manôÙuôÙfacôÙturôÙers to impleôÙment them. MoreôÙover, as yields are nevôÙer perôÙfect, the life cycle of plasôÙtics will not be infiôÙnite. FinalôÙly, even if they are no longer pureôÙly theôÙoôÙretôÙiôÙcal, these processôÙes are still being developed!

![](https://www.polytechnique-insights.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/03/adobestock_515640041-1024x768.jpeg)

One of the pioôÙneers of plasôÙtic degraôÙdaôÙtion using synôÙthetôÙic biolôÙoôÙgy is a French comôÙpaôÙny: CarôÙbios ([https://ãwwwã.carãbiosã.com/fr/](https://www.carbios.com/fr/)). It has develôÙoped a process based on an enzyme called LCC, idenôÙtiôÙfied in 2012 by JapanôÙese researchers who carôÙried out a metageôÙnomôÙic analyôÙsis ([https://ãwwwã.polyãtechãniqueã-insightsã.com/ãtãrãiãbãuãnãeãsã/ãsãaãnãtãeã-ãeãtã-ãbãiãoãtãeãcãhã/ãlãaã-ãmãeãtãaãgãeãnãoãmãiãqãuãeã-ãcãoãmãmãeãnãtã-ãeãtãuãdãiãeãrã-ãlãaã-ãbãiãoãdãiãvãeãrãsãiãtãeã-ãmãiãcãrãoãsãcãoãpique](https://www.polytechnique-insights.com/tribunes/sante-et-biotech/la-metagenomique-comment-etudier-la-biodiversite-microscopique)) of a comôÙpost[11](#note-11). ComôÙpared with othôÙer proôÙteins capaôÙble of degradôÙing PET, LCC was found to be parôÙticôÙuôÙlarôÙly effecôÙtive. MutaôÙtions were used to improve its activôÙiôÙty and temôÙperôÙaôÙture resisôÙtance to proôÙduce monomers that effecôÙtiveôÙly remanôÙuôÙfacôÙtured PET to a qualôÙiôÙty comôÙpaôÙraôÙble to new PET at a reaôÙsonôÙable cost[12](#note-12). After setôÙting up an indusôÙtriôÙal demonôÙstraôÙtor[13](#note-13), the comôÙpaôÙny conôÙtinôÙued its develôÙopôÙment with the conôÙstrucôÙtion of its first bioreôÙcyôÙcling site[14](#note-14).

## **Promises and limits**

Beyond this examôÙple, the numôÙber of patents linked to the recyôÙcling of plasôÙtics and the develôÙopôÙment of alterôÙnaôÙtive mateôÙriôÙals shows the dynamism of this secôÙtor[15](#note-15), which is of interôÙest to both indusôÙtry and funôÙdaôÙmenôÙtal research. Alone or in comôÙbiôÙnaôÙtion with othôÙer approachôÙes[16](#note-16), synôÙthetôÙic biolôÙoôÙgy is one of the tools that could make it posôÙsiôÙble to limôÙit the polôÙluôÙtion linked to the proôÙducôÙtion and use of plastics.

HowôÙevôÙer, this should not detract from the many issues raised by its use. Some are very clear, such as the manôÙageôÙment of addiôÙtives, the improveôÙment of yields, the optiôÙmiôÙsaôÙtion of costs, the adapôÙtaôÙtion to difôÙferôÙent types of plasôÙtics and, genôÙerôÙalôÙly, the difôÙfiôÙculôÙties of develôÙopôÙment and scalôÙing up. OthôÙers touch on more delôÙiôÙcate issues. Indeed, the micro-organôÙisms proôÙduced by synôÙthetôÙic biolôÙoôÙgy are genetôÙiôÙcalôÙly modôÙiôÙfied. This raisôÙes quesôÙtions about the patentabilôÙiôÙty of livôÙing organôÙisms, but also about the risks of release into the natôÙurôÙal enviôÙronôÙment. While the abilôÙiôÙty to degrade plasôÙtics is useôÙful in a waste manôÙageôÙment conôÙtext, it seems imporôÙtant to keep conôÙtrol ofô it.

In genôÙerôÙal, there is still a long way to go to achieve a reaôÙsonôÙable use of plasôÙtics. EstabôÙlishôÙing a true cirôÙcuôÙlar econôÙoôÙmy is a chalôÙlenge in itself[17](#note-17), reducôÙing our depenôÙdence on these ubiqôÙuiôÙtous mateôÙriôÙals is anothôÙer. While it is relôÙeôÙvant to conôÙsidôÙer each tool to move in the right direcôÙtion, no sinôÙgle tool will be sufôÙfiôÙcient to solve all the problems.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Synthetic biology techniques improve metabolic pathways in microbial strains, significantly increasing bioplastic yield and reducing production costs, thus enhancing overall efficiency (Source 1).

Advanced genetic engineering enhances the resilience of production strains against environmental stresses, ensuring consistent performance and stability during large-scale bioplastic production (Source 1).
Synthetic biology opens avenues for designing novel bioplastics with tailored properties, enabling the development of sustainable alternatives that meet diverse application needs (Source 5).
